title: CO-50 Insurance Denial: Not Medically Necessary — What It Means and How to Appeal
meta_description: CO-50 means your insurer denied your claim as not medically necessary. Learn what this means, what your rights are, and how to write a winning appeal.
target_problem: Patient received a CO-50 denial (not medically necessary) and needs to understand their rights and appeal options.
body:
Fewer than 1% of patients ever appeal a denied insurance claim — but of those who do, 40 to 60% win. If your insurer sent you a denial with code CO-50, you may have more options than you think.

CO-50 is a Claim Adjustment Reason Code (CARC) issued by insurance companies when they determine that a billed service was not medically necessary under your plan's coverage criteria.

Under federal law governing marketplace and employer-sponsored health plans, you have the right to receive a written explanation of why your claim was denied, request the insurer's coverage criteria, file an internal appeal, and request an Independent Medical Review if the internal appeal is denied.

How to Appeal: Request the denial letter and EOB. Ask your doctor for a Letter of Medical Necessity. Submit a formal internal appeal. If denied, request Independent Medical Review.

This content does not constitute legal or medical advice. MyAdvocate is not a law firm. For questions specific to your situation, consult a qualified attorney or healthcare professional.
